Tomato_Bacterial_spot.cause: A bacterial disease that spreads through infected seeds, splashing water, and humid conditions.
symptoms: Small dark brown or black spots on leaves
Yellowing around the spots
Spots may appear on stems and fruits
recommendations: Remove and destroy infected leaves to reduce spread.
Avoid overhead watering because splashing water spreads bacteria.
Use disease-free seeds or seedlings.
Maintain proper spacing between plants for airflow.
Use copper-based bactericide only as per local agricultural guidance.

Tomato_Early_blight.cause: A fungal disease commonly caused by Alternaria species, often favored by warm and humid conditions.
symptoms: Brown circular spots with concentric rings
Lower leaves become yellow and dry
Disease often starts from older leaves
recommendations: Remove affected lower leaves immediately.
Avoid wetting leaves while watering.
Use mulch to prevent soil splash.
Improve spacing and airflow.
Apply recommended fungicide only after expert/local guidance.

Tomato_Late_blight.cause: A serious water-mold disease favored by cool, wet, and humid weather.
symptoms: Large irregular dark patches on leaves
White mold-like growth may appear under leaves
Leaves and stems may collapse quickly
recommendations: Remove infected plants quickly because this disease spreads fast.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Keep the field well-ventilated.
Do not compost infected plant material.
Consult an agriculture expert for suitable fungicide treatment.

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.cause: A fungal disease that develops mainly in high humidity and poor air circulation.
symptoms: Yellow patches on upper leaf surface
Olive-green or gray mold under leaves
Leaves may curl, dry, and drop
recommendations: Reduce humidity around plants.
Increase spacing and ventilation.
Remove infected leaves.
Water at the base, not on leaves.
Use resistant varieties where possible.

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.cause: A fungal leaf spot disease that spreads through rain splash, infected debris, and humid weather.
symptoms: Many small circular spots on leaves
Spots often have gray centers and dark borders
Lower leaves turn yellow and drop
recommendations: Remove affected leaves and plant debris.
Avoid overhead watering.
Use mulch to reduce soil splash.
Maintain good spacing for airflow.
Rotate crops and avoid planting tomato repeatedly in the same soil.

Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.display_name: Tomato Spider Mites / Two-Spotted Spider Mite
Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.cause: A pest problem caused by tiny mites that suck plant sap, usually worse in hot and dry conditions.
symptoms: Tiny yellow or white specks on leaves
Fine webbing under leaves
Leaves may dry and look dusty
recommendations: Spray leaves with water to reduce mite population.
Remove badly affected leaves.
Avoid excessive dry conditions.
Encourage natural predators where possible.
Use miticide only with expert/local agricultural advice.

Tomato__Target_Spot.cause: A fungal disease that causes target-like lesions and spreads in warm, wet conditions.
symptoms: Brown spots with ring-like pattern
Leaf yellowing and defoliation
Can also affect stems and fruits
recommendations: Remove infected leaves.
Improve airflow through pruning and spacing.
Avoid overhead watering.
Keep field clean from infected debris.
Use approved fungicide only with agricultural guidance.

Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.cause: A viral disease mainly spread by whiteflies.
symptoms: Leaves curl upward
Yellowing of leaves
Stunted plant growth and reduced fruiting
recommendations: Remove severely infected plants to reduce spread.
Control whiteflies using yellow sticky traps and recommended practices.
Use insect-proof netting in nursery areas.
Remove weeds that may host whiteflies.
Use resistant tomato varieties where available.

Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: A viral disease that can spread through infected tools, hands, seeds, or plant contact.
symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves
Leaf curling or distortion
Reduced plant growth and poor fruit quality
recommendations: Remove infected plants carefully.
Disinfect tools and wash hands after touching infected plants.
Avoid smoking or tobacco contact near tomato plants.
Use certified disease-free seeds.
Avoid working with plants when leaves are wet.

Tomato_healthy.cause: No disease detected by the model.
symptoms: Leaf appears normal and healthy
recommendations: Continue regular monitoring.
Water at the base of the plant.
Maintain proper spacing and sunlight.
Remove weeds and dead leaves.
Use preventive care instead of unnecessary chemical treatment.
